text.title: MCP Security — Consent, Injection Defenses, Access Controls — Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every application of `mcp-security` must enforce. Verifiable at review time; failures gate the artifact.

rule.statement: Every first-time tool call requires explicit user consent in a distinguishable UI surface; consent cached per-session at most.

rationale.source: MCP spec; Practical DevSecOps MCP Security 2026

rule.rationale: Implicit-consent UX defeats MCP's primary safety control.

rule.statement: Remote MCP servers MUST enforce OAuth 2.1 with PKCE; no anonymous remote calls.
rule.rationale: Unauthenticated remote MCP exposes tools to anyone reaching the endpoint.

rule.statement: Tool output is DATA, not instructions; clients sanitise / flag suspicious tokens; never inline raw into the next prompt.

rationale.source: Simon Willison MCP prompt injection 2025; arxiv 2601.17549

rule.rationale: Tool-output prompt injection is the #1 MCP attack vector in 2026.

rule.statement: Server capabilities are explicitly declared + verifiable; clients reject undeclared methods.
rule.rationale: Absence of capability attestation lets servers claim arbitrary permissions.

rule.statement: Multi-server configurations require explicit per-server isolation; no implicit trust propagation.
rule.rationale: Implicit trust across servers enables cross-server escalation.

rule.statement: Every tool call is logged with server_id + tool_name + caller_session + arguments_hash + outcome + timestamp.
rule.rationale: Without audit logs, post-incident attribution and forensics are impossible.

rule.statement: Maintain a server allow-list + cryptographic identity (signed manifest or pinned cert); reject servers that don't verify.
rule.rationale: Lookalike-server replacement attacks substitute a malicious server with the same name.
